perturbation.original_text: The PC shall employ and control the chiropractors and other chiropractic personnel that will provide the actual chiropractic services required to be delivered at and through the Clinic. You shall not provide any actual chiropractic services, nor shall you supervise, direct, control or suggest to, the PC or its chiropractors or employees the manner in which the PC provides or may provide chiropractic services to its patients.
perturbation.changed_text: The PC shall employ the chiropractors and other chiropractic personnel that will provide the actual chiropractic services required to be delivered at and through the Clinic. You shall supervise, direct, control or suggest to, the PC or its chiropractors or employees the manner in which the PC provides or may provide chiropractic services to its patients.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts standard regulations about corporate practice of medicine and scope of practice, particularly concerning non-licensed individuals directing healthcare professionals.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Corporate Practice of Medicine Doctrine and Scope of Practice Regulations
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state, example: California Business and Professions Code § 2052
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text explicitly prevents the non-licensed franchisee from controlling chiropractic service delivery, aligning with corporate practice of medicine doctrines. The modified text directly contradicts this, potentially violating state laws prohibiting unlicensed individuals from practicing medicine or directing medical practice.
perturbation.location: 2.3 Management Agreement with Professional Corporation - Non-Licensed Franchisees

HEALING ARTS [500 - 4999.129] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 399. ) CHAPTER 5. Medicine [2000 - 2529.8.1] ( Chapter 5 repealed and added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1313, Sec. 2. ) ARTICLE 3. License Required and Exemptions [2050 - 2078] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1313, Sec. 2. ) 2052. (a) Notwithstanding Section 146, any person who practices or attempts to practice, or who advertises or holds himself or herself out as practicing, any system or mode of treating the sick or afflicted in this state, or who diagnoses, treats, operates for, or prescribes for any ailment, blemish, deformity, disease, disfigurement, disorder, injury, or other physical or mental condition of any person, without having at the time of so doing a valid, unrevoked, or unsuspended certificate as provided in this chapter or without being authorized to perform the act pursuant to a certificate obtained in accordance with some other provision of law is guilty of a public offense, punishable by a fine not exceeding ten thousand dollars ($10,000), by imprisonment pursuant to subdivision (h) of Section 1170 of the Penal Code, by imprisonment in a county jail not exceeding one year, or by both the fine and either imprisonment. (b) Any person
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle that unlicensed individuals should not control or direct the practice of licensed healthcare professionals. The original text prevented this, but the changed text explicitly allows the non-licensed party to 'supervise, direct, control or suggest' how chiropractic services are provided. This is a clear violation of BPC 2052(a), which prohibits the practice of medicine without a valid certificate. The change undermines the legal requirement for licensed professionals to exercise independent judgment in patient care.

perturbation.original_text: The person or entity to whom you wish to make the Transfer, or its principal owners ("Proposed New Owner"), must be individuals of good moral character and otherwise meet our then-applicable standards for The Joint Corp. Location franchisees.
perturbation.changed_text: The person or entity to whom you wish to make the Transfer, or its principal owners ("Proposed New Owner"), must be entities of questionable character and meet standards set by the current owner for The Joint Corp. Location franchisees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'good moral character' to 'questionable character' directly opposes typical franchise transfer requirements which mandate suitable and reputable owners.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: This directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing inherent in most contracts. Requiring a transferee of 'questionable character' makes the contract unconscionable and unenforceable as it undermines the stability and reputation of the franchise system.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation. By requiring a transferee of 'questionable character,' the modification undermines the stability and reputation of the franchise system. This violates the principle of good faith in contract performance, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The change from 'good moral character' to 'questionable character' is a significant deviation that would likely render the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All advertising you employ must be completely factual, in good taste (in our judgment), and conform to the highest standards of ethical advertising and all legal requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: All advertising you employ is encouraged to be misleading, shocking (in our judgment), and disregard all standards of ethical advertising and all legal requirements.
perturbation.explanation: Reversing the requirement of factual, ethical, and legal advertising directly contradicts advertising laws and regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause adheres to advertising laws. The modified text advocates for deceptive practices, which directly violates the FTC Act prohibiting unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. This makes the clause illegal and creates potential liability for both the franchisee and franchisor.
perturbation.location: 10.6 Compliance with Laws and Good Business Practices

Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition i
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts advertising laws. The original text requires advertising to be factual, ethical, and legal. The changed text encourages misleading, shocking, and unethical advertising, disregarding all legal requirements. The FTC Act, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, empowers the Commission to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.' Promoting misleading advertising is a direct violation of this act. Additionally, 15 USC 45, as noted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, prohibits 'Unfair methods of competition unlawful,' which deceptive advertising clearly falls under. Thus, the modified clause establishes a contradiction by advocating for practices explicitly prohibited by federal law.
